bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Rev. David Brainerd (1718-1747), missionary to the Delaware Indians.

narrative_blocks.header: Expelled from Yale for criticizing a tutor
narrative_blocks.body: In 1742 Brainerd was expelled from Yale after saying a tutor 'has no more grace than a chair.' His expulsion helped drive the founding of the College of New Jersey (later Princeton) in 1746.

narrative_blocks.header: Missionary to the Delaware Indians, 77 baptisms
narrative_blocks.body: After being licensed by the New Lights in 1742, he served as missionary to the Mahican at Kaunaumeek, then to the Delaware in Pennsylvania, then in New Jersey at Crossweeksung. A sudden outpouring of the Spirit there brought 77 baptisms in less than a year.

narrative_blocks.header: Nursed by Jerusha Edwards in her father's home
narrative_blocks.body: Tuberculosis-stricken, he reached Jonathan Edwards's home in Northampton on May 28, 1747. Jerusha Edwards, age seventeen, devoted herself to nursing him until his death October 9 — contracting the disease that would kill her four months later.

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards published his diary; bestseller for a century
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Edwards edited and published Brainerd's diary in 1749 as 'An Account of the Life of the Late Reverend Mr. Brainerd.' It became the best-selling religious book of nineteenth-century America, inspiring missionaries William Carey, Henry Martyn, and Adoniram Judson.

cross_connections.display_label: Engaged to her; she nursed him until his death October 1747
Died in his home; he edited and published Brainerd's diary
A Connecticut baby named for him was born 1747

sources.label: Salt of the Earth — David Brainerd and Jerusha Edwards: A Colonial Love Story

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST023.name: Hopkins Academy
INST023.notes: Hadley, Massachusetts academy founded from the bequest of Edward Hopkins — the same bequest behind Hopkins Grammar School, New Haven (INST001), but a DISTINCT institution. Roger Hooker Leavitt and Hart Leavitt are graduates; compiler flagged the distinction Stage 6Y-687.
